chunks.text: Income Tax in Ghana is governed by the Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896) as amended by Act 941 (2016), Act 956 (2017) and Act 1,007 (2020). The Act replaced the old Internal Revenue Act 2000 (Act 592). A resident individual is taxed on worldwide income whereas a non-resident is only taxed on Ghana-source income. Residency is determined based on domicile, stay of 183 days or more in any 12-month period, or being an employee or official of the Government of Ghana posted abroad.

Tax rates for individuals are progressive ranging from 0% on the first GHS 5,880 of annual income to 35% on income above GHS 240,000 per year as at the latest amendment — always verify current bands at gra.gov.gh because the budget updates them. Self-employed individuals must file a tax return by 30 April of the following year. Employment income is taxed at source via the PAYE system.
Investment income such as dividends, interest and rent may be subject to withholding tax which can be final or credited depending on the payer and nature.

Q: who pays income tax in Ghana? A: Every individual who earns income in Ghana is potentially subject to income tax. This includes salaried employees, self-employed persons, sole proprietors, partners in a partnership, and residents earning foreign income. Exemptions exist for certain incomes e.g. income of a person under a scholarship, pension income up to certain thresholds, gratuity and terminal benefits capped at a quarter of the total emoluments, gains from life insurance policies and certain agricultural income.

Q: what is chargeable income? A: Chargeable income is the amount of income on which you actually pay tax after deducting allowable reliefs and deductions from your total income. Total income minus allowable deductions equals chargeable income.

Pay As You Earn (PAYE) is the mechanism by which employers deduct income tax from employees salaries and wages at source and remit to GRA. An employer is required to register as a withholding agent with GRA. PAYE is calculated on the employee's monthly chargeable income after deducting mandatory Social Security (SSNIT) contributions of 5.5% and any applicable personal reliefs. The employer deducts PAYE every month and must file a monthly PAYE return and remit the tax by the 15th of the following month.

Failure to remit attracts a penalty of 10% of the tax due plus interest at the prevailing Bank of Ghana rate. At year-end, the employer issues each employee a P9 form (certificate of emoluments and PAYE deducted) which the employee uses for their annual tax return if required. An employee with multiple employers must disclose this to both employers and to GRA to ensure correct total PAYE.
An employee who earns only employment income and has had the correct PAYE deducted is not required to file a separate annual return unless GRA specifically requests one.

PAYE rates for monthly income (approximate, always confirm at gra.gov.gh): First GHS 490 per month — 0%. Next GHS 110 — 5%. Next GHS 130 — 10%. Next GHS 3,000 — 17.5%. Next GHS 16,270 — 25%. Next GHS 20,000 — 30%. Exceeding GHS 20,000 monthly — 35%. Note: these bands are indexed to the national budget and may change annually. An employer who fails to deduct PAYE becomes personally liable for the undeducted tax. Expatriate employees working in Ghana are also subject to PAYE on their Ghana-source employment income regardless of where their salary is paid. Allowances such as housing, transport, utility and entertainment allowances paid to an employee are included in employment income for PAYE purposes unless specifically exempt under the Act.

Companies operating in Ghana pay Corporate Income Tax (CIT) on their chargeable income. The standard CIT rate is 25%. However the following special rates apply: Companies engaged in non-traditional exports — 8%. Hotels and companies in the tourism sector — 22%. Financial institutions including banks — 35% (reduced to 25% for community banks and rural banks). Mining companies — 35%. Petroleum companies — variable under the applicable Petroleum Agreement, often around 35% plus additional oil entitlement.

Free Zone enterprises — 0% for the first 10 years and 15% thereafter. Agricultural businesses — 0% for the first 5 years subject to conditions, then 20%. Companies located in regional capitals outside Accra and Kumasi — 18.75% for 5 years. Companies in districts other than regional capitals — 12.5% for 5 years. Manufacturing companies — 25%. Real estate developers — 22% on speculative building profits. CIT return must be filed within 4 months after year-end (April 30 for December year-end companies).

Corporate Tax Filing Process in Ghana. 
Step 1: Register your company with GRA and obtain a TIN if not already done. 
Step 2: Keep proper books of account in Ghana cedis throughout the year. 
Step 3: Calculate provisional tax in four equal installments and pay by the last working day of March, June, September and December (for December year-end). 
Step 4: After year-end, prepare financial statements in accordance with IFRS or IFRS for SMEs as applicable.

Step 5: Complete the annual self-assessment return (Form ITPF 7A for companies) and file with the relevant GRA tax office by 30 April. 
Step 6: Pay any balance of tax due when filing. Interest accrues on underpayments of provisional tax if underpayment exceeds 10% of the final liability. A company that changes its accounting year must obtain GRA approval. Small companies (turnover below GHS 20,000 per year) may qualify for the small business rate — verify current thresholds as these change.

Withholding Tax (WHT) in Ghana acts as an advance payment of tax or as a final tax depending on the type of payment. Payments subject to WHT include: Dividends — 8% final tax (resident and non-resident). Interest paid by financial institutions to individuals — 8% final tax. Interest on Government securities — 0%. Rent paid to individuals — 8% final tax; rent paid to companies — 8% creditable. Service fees to resident individuals — 7.5% creditable. Service fees to resident companies — 7.5% creditable.

Service fees to non-residents — 20% final tax. Management fees to non-residents — 20% final tax. Royalties to residents — 15% creditable. Royalties to non-residents — 20% final tax. Natural resource payments — 15%. Lottery winnings above a threshold — 5% final tax. Endorsement fees — 10% final tax. Sports persons and entertainers — 15% final tax. The withholder must file a WHT return and remit by the 15th of the following month.
A WHT certificate must be issued to the recipient which they use to credit against their annual tax liability (for creditable WHT).

Q: Do I have to withhold tax on payments to foreign companies? A: Yes. Payments to non-resident persons for services rendered in Ghana are subject to 20% WHT which is a final tax. This covers management fees, technical fees, consulting fees, royalties, interest (at 8%) and dividends (at 8%). 

Q: What happens if I forget to withhold? A: The payer/withholder becomes personally liable for the tax that should have been withheld. GRA may assess the payer for the undeducted WHT plus interest and penalties.

Q: Can the non-resident get a refund if there is a Double Taxation Agreement? A: Yes, Ghana has DTAs with several countries including UK, France, Germany, South Africa, Belgium, Netherlands, Italy, Singapore, Switzerland, Czech Republic and Mauritius. Where a DTA provides a reduced WHT rate the non-resident can apply to GRA for relief.

Capital Gains Tax (CGT) in Ghana is charged on gains from the realisation of investment assets. Investment assets include land and buildings, business assets, shares, unit trust interests and other financial assets. CGT rate: 15% on gains from realisation of assets by resident persons. A gain is calculated as proceeds minus the cost base of the asset. Cost base includes original acquisition cost, improvement expenditure and incidental costs of acquisition.

Proceeds include all amounts received, including the FMV of non-cash consideration. Exemptions: Gain on disposal of a principal private residence owned and occupied for at least three years — exempt. Gain on transfer between spouses — exempt (no gain/no loss). Gain on reorganisations within the same group of companies — may be deferred subject to conditions. Shares listed on the Ghana Stock Exchange — gains are exempt (to encourage investment in capital markets).
CGT return must be filed and tax paid within 30 days of realisation. Failure to report is an offence.

Gift Tax in Ghana. Under the Income Tax Act 2015 (Act 896), gifts received by individuals are included in the income of the recipient and taxed accordingly. A gift means the receipt of money or moneys worth without adequate consideration. Gifts from employers are treated as employment income. Gifts from family members and from religious organisations in the course of religious activities are generally exempt. Gifts received in course of a business are treated as business income not gift tax. The value of a gift is its market value at the date of receipt. A recipient of a taxable gift must declare it in their annual income tax return. The rate applicable to gifts is the marginal income tax rate of the recipient. Where the gift is land or building, stamp duty implications also arise — that is administered by the Lands Commission not GRA. The giver of a gift does not pay gift tax but if the gift involves an asset disposal, CGT may arise for the giver.

Value Added Tax (VAT) in Ghana is governed by the Value Added Tax Act 2013 (Act 870) as amended. The standard VAT rate is 15%. Additionally the National Health Insurance Levy (NHIL) of 2.5% and the Ghana Education Trust Fund Levy (GETFund Levy) of 2.5% are charged alongside VAT, bringing the effective rate on standard-rated supplies to 21.9% when the COVID-19 Health Recovery Levy (1%) is included.

Registration threshold: A person who makes taxable supplies exceeding GHS 200,000 in any 12-month period must register for VAT. Voluntary registration is allowed for persons making or intending to make taxable supplies below the threshold. Application for registration is made online at the GRA taxpayer portal or in person at a GRA VAT office.

The registered person must issue tax invoices for all taxable supplies, file monthly VAT returns by the last working day of the following month, and remit any net VAT due. Input VAT (VAT paid on business purchases) can be offset against output VAT (VAT charged on sales). Flat Rate Scheme: Retailers and others meeting the criteria may use the VAT Flat Rate Scheme of 3% on turnover instead of the standard method.

Q: What goods and services are exempt from VAT in Ghana? A: Exempt supplies include: unprocessed foodstuffs grown or caught in Ghana, agricultural inputs such as fertilisers seeds and pesticides, financial services (not fees), educational services, medical and healthcare services, residential accommodation rental, public passenger transport, supply of water by GWCL, supply of electricity in domestic quantities, and books and educational material. Zero-rated supplies (taxable at 0%, meaning VAT registered suppliers can still claim input VAT) include: exported goods and services, international transport of goods and passengers, supply of crude oil and refined petroleum for export.

Q: What is a Tax Invoice? A: A VAT tax invoice must show the supplier name and address, TIN, VAT registration number, date, sequential invoice number, description of supply, taxable value, VAT amount, NHIL and GETFund amounts and totals. Without a proper tax invoice, a buyer cannot claim input VAT credit.

The VAT Flat Rate Scheme (VFRS) in Ghana applies to eligible retailers. Under VFRS the trader charges 3% VAT on the selling price of goods and remits the full 3% to GRA — there is no input VAT deduction. This simplifies accounting for small businesses. Eligibility: turnover between GHS 200,000 and GHS 500,000 per annum, primarily selling goods (not services), not producing goods. Businesses that primarily provide services must use the standard VAT method. Businesses above GHS 500,000 must switch to standard VAT. VFRS businesses must still issue receipts and file monthly returns. The 3% is charged on top of the selling price so technically it is 3/103 of the gross amount — in practice most traders charge 3% on the original price. NHIL and GETFund levies do not apply to the VFRS — only the 3% is charged and remitted.

VAT compliance obligations summary for registered businesses. 1. Issue tax invoices for every taxable supply. 2. Keep copies of all invoices issued and received for at least 6 years. 3. File monthly VAT return (Form VAT 1A) by the last working day of each month for the previous month. 4. Pay net VAT due at the time of filing. 5. Apply for a VAT certificate which must be displayed at your business premises. 6. Notify GRA within 21 days of any change in business details (address, directors, nature of business). 7. File a nil return even if no transactions occurred in a month. Penalties for late filing: GHS 500 per month for each month of default. Penalties for non-payment: 10% of the tax due plus interest at Bank of Ghana rate. Businesses that cease trading must notify GRA and apply for deregistration. Fraudulent claiming of input VAT is a criminal offence under Act 870 and can result in prosecution.

Tax Identification Number (TIN) Registration in Ghana. A TIN is a unique 11-digit number assigned to every taxpayer by GRA. It is mandatory for: opening a bank account, registering a business, clearing goods at the port, purchasing land or immovable property, applying for a driver licence, registering a vehicle, obtaining a building permit, applying for government procurement contracts, and many other official transactions.

Who must register: every individual earning income in Ghana (employed or self-employed), every company or partnership, every NGO and charity, every public institution. How to register: Individuals — visit taxpayerportal.gra.gov.gh and select TIN self-service, or visit any GRA office. You need a valid Ghana Card (National ID), or passport for non-Ghanaians. Companies — apply online or in person with Certificate of Incorporation, Certificate to Commence Business, Form 3 and Form 4 from Registrar General.
Processing time: TIN is issued on the spot at offices or within 24–48 hours online. TIN registration is FREE — do not pay anyone to register for you.

Q: I lost my TIN card, how do I get a replacement? A: Visit any GRA office or log into your account at taxpayerportal.gra.gov.gh. You can print a TIN certificate from the portal or request a replacement card at the office with your ID. 

Q: I have multiple TINs from the past. Is that a problem? A: Yes, you should only have one TIN. Multiple TINs create compliance issues. Visit GRA to merge or deactivate duplicate TINs.

Q: Is a TIN the same as my SSNIT number? A: No. Your SSNIT number is assigned by SSNIT (Social Security and National Insurance Trust) for pension purposes. Your TIN is assigned by GRA for tax purposes. They are different numbers but both may be needed for employment. 

Q: Do children need a TIN? A: Generally no, unless the child earns income such as endorsement fees, acting income or investment income. Parents who invest in the child's name should consult GRA on registration requirements.
Q: Can a foreigner get a TIN in Ghana? A: Yes. Non-Ghanaian individuals working or earning income in Ghana must register for a TIN using their passport and work/residence permit.

Personal Tax Reliefs available to individuals in Ghana (confirm current amounts at gra.gov.gh as these are updated annually). Marriage Relief: GHS 1,200 per year — available to married persons. Child/Dependent Relief: GHS 1,200 per child up to maximum 2 children — available for legitimate children or dependents in full-time education. Disability Relief: 25% of assessable income — for persons with disability who hold a valid disability card.

Old Age Relief: GHS 1,500 per year — for persons aged 60 and above who are still working. Aged Dependant Relief: GHS 1,000 per year for each dependant aged 60 and above (maximum 2 dependants). Educational Relief: Actual cost of training up to a maximum allowed by GRA — for professional development related to employment. Cost of premises used for business from home — proportion of home expenses used for business. Life insurance relief: actual premium paid up to 10% of assessable income.
Social Security (SSNIT) contribution: mandatory employee contribution is a relief automatically deducted from assessable income. All reliefs must be claimed on the annual tax return (IT1) form.

Q: My employer does not process my personal reliefs in the PAYE. What should I do? A: Submit a completed Relief Application Form (IT 1) to your employer and request they process the reliefs going forward. Your employer is required to process reliefs you validly claim. If they refuse, contact GRA. You may also claim reliefs retrospectively in your annual tax return. 

Q: Can both husband and wife claim marriage relief? A: No. Only one spouse can claim the marriage relief.

Q: I am a person with disability. Do I still pay income tax? A: Yes but you are entitled to a 25% reduction on your assessable income as a relief. You also need a valid NSDA (National Service Disability Allowance) card or equivalent government-issued disability card. 

Q: I support my elderly parents. Can I claim aged dependant relief for them? A: Yes, provided they are 60 or older and genuinely dependent on you financially. You can claim up to 2 aged dependants.

Self-Employed Persons and Tax in Ghana. If you run your own business, work as a freelancer, or earn income outside of formal employment, you are self-employed for tax purposes. You must: register for a TIN if you have not already, keep records of income and expenses, file an annual self-assessment return (Form IT1) by 30 April each year for the previous year, and pay any tax due. Provisional tax: You must also make quarterly provisional tax payments on estimated income to avoid interest charges.

Provisional tax payments fall due on the last day of March, June, September and December. Allowable deductions for self-employed: Cost of goods sold. Business-related salaries and wages. Rent of business premises. Interest on business loans. Depreciation (capital allowance) on business assets. Business insurance. Utilities and communications for business. Professional fees (legal, accounting) for business. Motor vehicle expenses to the extent used for business.
Charitable donations to approved charities (limited deduction). Expenses must be wholly, exclusively and necessarily for business to be deductible.

Q: I am a freelance graphic designer. Do I charge my clients VAT? A: If your annual revenue exceeds GHS 200,000 you must register for VAT and charge VAT on your fees. Below this threshold VAT registration is optional. 

Q: I drive an Uber. Am I self-employed for tax? A: Yes. Ride-hailing drivers are considered self-employed. Income from Uber, Bolt or similar platforms is taxable. You should register for a TIN and file annual returns declaring your earnings.

Q: Can I deduct fuel costs as a driver? A: Yes, fuel, vehicle maintenance, insurance, and depreciation of your vehicle (capital allowance) used for income-earning purposes are allowable deductions. 

Q: I am a photographer at weddings. How much tax do I pay? A: Your net profit (income minus allowable expenses) is subject to income tax at the individual progressive rates. The effective rate depends on how much profit you make.

Q: I earn income from YouTube. Is that taxable in Ghana? A: Yes. Digital income, including YouTube ad revenue, social media income and online freelancing income earned by Ghanaian residents, is taxable under the Income Tax Act.

Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) Tax Obligations in Ghana. GRA defines a small taxpayer as one with annual turnover not exceeding GHS 20,000 (verify current threshold as this is updated periodically). Turnover Tax: Small taxpayers who cannot maintain proper books of account may opt for the Turnover Tax regime. Under Turnover Tax, the business pays 3% of annual turnover instead of the standard income tax on profits. This is simpler but may be more expensive if margins are thin.

A business wishing to leave the Turnover Tax regime needs GRA approval. Medium businesses with turnover between the small threshold and the VAT registration threshold have standard income tax obligations (file return, pay tax on profits) but may not yet need VAT registration. SMEs are urged to keep basic records: Cash book. Sales records. Purchase records. Stock records. These records help compute taxable profit correctly and defend against estimated assessments by GRA.
GRA has SME Desks at major offices dedicated to helping small businesses understand their obligations.

NGOs (Non-Governmental Organisations) and Tax in Ghana. NGOs are not automatically exempt from all taxes. They must register with GRA and obtain a TIN. Income Tax: An NGO established for charitable, educational, religious or social welfare purposes may apply for exemption from income tax under Section 97 of the Income Tax Act, 2015. The exemption is not automatic — it must be applied for and approved by the Commissioner-General of GRA.

The NGO must demonstrate that: it is not operated for profit, income is applied solely to the stated objectives, no benefit flows to individual members. Exemption must be renewed periodically. Employment income of NGO staff: still subject to PAYE — the exemption does not cover employment income. VAT: NGOs that provide taxable services or sell taxable goods above the registration threshold must register for VAT.

WHT: NGOs are required to withhold tax on payments to contractors and service providers just like any other entity. Import Duties: Some NGOs with specific government agreements may be exempt from import duties — this is granted by the Ministry of Finance not GRA. Filing: Even with income tax exemption, NGOs must file annual information returns with GRA.

Tax on Real Estate Transactions in Ghana. Sale of property: Capital Gains Tax at 15% applies to gains from sale of land or buildings. The gain is proceeds minus cost base. If the property is your principal private residence and you have lived there for at least 3 years, the gain is exempt. The CGT return must be filed and tax paid within 30 days of completion of the sale. Rental income: Rental income is taxable income. Withholding tax of 8% is deducted by the tenant if the landlord is an individual.

This WHT is a final tax for individuals — the landlord does not need to include the rental income in their return if WHT has been correctly deducted. If the landlord is a company, the 8% WHT is creditable against the company's CIT. Multi-let properties: The landlord must issue receipts and keep records of all rental income. Property developers: Companies developing properties for sale are taxed at 22% on profits.

Stamp Duty: Applies on transfer of property deeds — this is a separate levy administered by the Lands Commission at rates ranging from 0.5% to 2% of property value. GRA and Lands Commission share information on property transactions.

Tax Regime for Mining Companies in Ghana. Mining companies are subject to a higher CIT rate of 35%. In addition, mining companies pay: Royalties to the Government of Ghana at rates negotiated in the Mineral Development Agreement (typically 3% to 6% of gross revenue from minerals). Mineral royalties are an allowable deduction for CIT purposes. Additional Profits Tax (Windfall Tax): applicable when returns exceed a set threshold — this has been introduced and revised several times.

Capital allowances: Mine development and capital expenditure is deductible over the useful life of the asset using the reducing balance or straight-line method as specified. Ring fencing: Each mining lease is treated as a separate entity for tax purposes — losses from one mine cannot be offset against profits from another mine. Stability agreements: Some older mining companies have fiscal stability clauses in their Investment Agreements locking in tax rates for a set period.

Oil and Gas: Petroleum companies are subject to the Petroleum Income Tax Act and their specific Petroleum Agreements. Typical rates: CIT 35%, Additional Oil Entitlement (AOE) varies, and Petroleum income tax provisions apply to ring-fenced petroleum operations.

Communications Service Tax (CST) in Ghana. CST is levied under the Communications Service Tax Act 2008 (Act 754) as amended. Rate: 9% on the charges for use of communications services. Who pays: end users of telecommunications services including voice calls, SMS, data services, and electronic communications. The tax is collected by the communications service provider (telco) and remitted to GRA.

Services covered: voice calls on mobile and fixed networks, SMS, data bundles, pay-per-use internet, electronic mail, electronic messaging platforms (OTT services such as WhatsApp calls). The telco must file a monthly CST return and remit by the 15th of the following month. The CST is embedded in the cost of airtime top-up and data bundles — customers pay it automatically. Exemptions: international incoming calls routed to Ghana are not subject to CST.
Revenue sharing arrangements between telcos and content providers: the CST applies to the gross charge to the end customer, not just the telco's share.

Import and Customs Duties in Ghana. Customs duties are administered by the Customs Division of GRA at ports of entry. Ghana applies the ECOWAS Common External Tariff (CET) which classifies goods under the Harmonized System (HS Codes) with duty rates of 0%, 5%, 10%, 20% and 35% depending on the type of good. In addition to import duty, importers pay: Import VAT (15%+), NHIL (2.5%), GETFund Levy (2.5%), ECOWAS Levy (0.5%), African Union Levy (0.2%), EXIM Levy (0.5%), and inspection fees.

The total effective rate on many goods can be 40% to 60% of the CIF (Cost, Insurance and Freight) value. Import process: Importers must register with GRA Customs, have a TIN, and appoint a licensed Customs House Broker to process declarations. The Ghana Integrated Cargo Management System (GICMS) / UNIPASS is used for electronic declarations.

Classification disputes: If an importer disputes the duty rate applied by Customs, they can appeal to the Commissioner of Customs and subsequently to the Revenue Appeals Board. Prohibited imports: Certain goods are prohibited from import entirely (e.g. counterfeit goods, certain chemical weapons precursors). Restricted imports require special permits (e.g. firearms, pharmaceuticals, food items).

Q: How is customs duty calculated? A: Customs duty is calculated on the CIF value of goods (cost of goods + insurance + freight to Ghana port). The CIF value is multiplied by the applicable duty rate. 

Q: I am importing personal effects after returning from abroad. Do I pay duty? A: Returning residents and citizens may apply for relief on used personal effects and household items when relocating permanently to Ghana. The items must have been owned and used abroad for at least 6 months.

Q: What is a Benchmark Value? A: GRA Customs uses benchmark (reference) values for certain goods to prevent under-invoicing. If the declared value is below the benchmark, Customs will use the benchmark value to calculate duty. Importers can challenge benchmark values. 

Q: Can I pay customs duty in installments? A: Generally no — full payment is required for release of goods. However in specific circumstances a bond or security may be accepted.

Q: What is the difference between customs duty and excise duty? A: Customs duty is charged on imported goods at the border. Excise duty is charged on specific locally-produced or imported goods (e.g. alcoholic beverages, tobacco, fuel, plastics) and is administered separately by GRA's Domestic Tax Revenue Division.

Excise Duty in Ghana is levied under the Excise Duty Act 2014 (Act 878). Excise duty applies to the manufacture and importation of specified goods including: alcoholic beverages (beer, spirits, wine), tobacco products, soft drinks and non-alcoholic beverages, plastics and plastic products, textiles, petroleum products, vehicle/automotive parts (selected). Rates vary by product type.

For example: beer — GHS per litre or percentage of factory price; spirits — higher rate per litre; tobacco products — per stick or percentage. Manufacturers of excisable goods must register with GRA, install GRA-approved excise meters or stamps (tax stamps on cigarette packs and alcohol), file monthly returns and pay excise duty on goods removed from the factory. Tax stamps: Ghana uses a track-and-trace tax stamp system for tobacco and alcoholic beverages to combat illicit trade.

All cigarette packs and alcohol bottles sold in Ghana must bear a valid GRA tax stamp. Buying or selling unstamped tobacco or alcohol is an offence. Excise duty is charged in addition to other taxes like VAT.

Summary of key GRA filing deadlines. Monthly obligations (by 15th of following month): PAYE, WHT returns and remittances, CST returns. Monthly obligations (last working day of following month): VAT returns and payments. Quarterly: Provisional income tax payments for individuals and companies — last day of March, June, September, December. Annual income tax return for individuals: 30 April (self-employed, rental income earners, persons with multiple income sources).

Annual income tax return for companies: within 4 months of year-end. For December year-end companies this is 30 April. VAT annual adjustment for partial exemption businesses: within 30 days of year-end. CGT return and payment: within 30 days of disposal. Nil returns: A taxpayer registered for a tax type must file a nil return even in periods of no activity. The GRA online portal at taxpayerportal.gra.gov.gh allows online filing for most tax types.

Filing can also be done in person at any GRA district office. GRA may grant extensions of time to file on written application with good cause — but extension of filing does not extend the time to pay.

How to File Taxes Online with GRA. The GRA Taxpayer Portal is at taxpayerportal.gra.gov.gh. 
Step 1: Go to taxpayerportal.gra.gov.gh and click on Register if you are a new user, or Login if you already have an account. 
Step 2: Register using your TIN, personal details and create a password. 
Step 3: After logging in, select the tax type you want to file (Income Tax, VAT, PAYE, WHT etc.). 
Step 4: Select the period you are filing for and complete the return form online.

Step 5: Review your submission and confirm. 
Step 6: Proceed to make payment using the options available (bank transfer, GhIPSS, Mobile Money, card payment at partner banks).

Step 7: Download and save your acknowledgement receipt and payment confirmation. Common issues: Portal login problems — try resetting password or call the GRA helpline 0800-900-110. TIN not recognised — you may need to complete registration at a GRA office first. Portal is available 24/7 but maintenance windows may apply. For large companies, the GRA Large Taxpayer Office (LTO) handles filings for taxpayers above a certain revenue threshold — check with GRA if your company qualifies.

Tax Clearance Certificate (TCC) in Ghana. A TCC is issued by GRA as evidence that a taxpayer is compliant with their tax obligations — all returns are filed and all taxes are paid or a payment plan is in place. When is a TCC required? For government procurement contracts above a threshold. For renewal of business licences with various government agencies. For applications to the Public Procurement Authority. For obtaining licences from Bank of Ghana, Securities Exchange Commission and other regulators.

For some visa and travel applications. For import and export licences. For settlement of deceased estates. How to obtain a TCC: Ensure all outstanding returns are filed and taxes paid. Apply online at the GRA portal or in person at your tax office. GRA verifies compliance across all tax types (income tax, PAYE, VAT, WHT). A TCC is usually valid for one year. A taxpayer with an agreed installment plan may still qualify for a TCC — GRA has discretion.
Fake TCCs: GRA has an online verification tool to check the authenticity of a TCC. Any person who obtains or uses a fake TCC commits an offence.

Penalties and Interest for Non-Compliance in Ghana. Late filing penalty: For individuals and companies — GHS 500 per month for each month of default (verify current rate as amendments apply). Late payment penalty: 10% of the tax due. Interest on outstanding tax: Simple interest at the Bank of Ghana monetary policy rate plus 5% per annum. Failure to register for a tax type: GHS 5,000 for companies, GHS 1,000 for individuals (approximate — verify). Failure to keep records: up to GHS 1,000 per offence.

Obstruction of a GRA officer: criminal offence. Tax evasion (intentional non-disclosure or fraud): criminal prosecution, imprisonment up to 5 years, and a penalty up to 3 times the tax evaded. Failure to withhold: personal liability for the undeducted amount plus penalties. Using a false TIN: criminal offence. Understatement of income: additional assessment plus 50% penalty on the understatement. GRA may remit penalties in cases of genuine hardship — taxpayers can apply in writing.
Where there is an amnesty programme (which GRA has periodically announced), penalties and interest may be waived subject to conditions.

How to Apply for Penalty Waiver or Interest Remission. GRA may consider waiving penalties and interest in genuine cases of hardship or error. To apply: 
Step 1: Ensure all outstanding tax principal (actual tax owed) is paid first or a payment plan is agreed — GRA will not waive penalties on unpaid principal.

Step 2: Write a formal letter to the Commissioner of the relevant division (Domestic Tax, Customs etc.) stating the reasons for the waiver request, the circumstances leading to non-compliance, and any supporting documents. 
Step 3: Attach evidence of payment of the principal tax. 
Step 4: Submit the letter and documents at the relevant GRA office or through your relationship manager if you have one.

Step 5: GRA will review and communicate a decision in writing. Waiver applications are discretionary — there is no guarantee. Factors that help: first-time non-compliance, genuine financial difficulty, natural disaster or emergency, death of the taxpayer/director, or a GRA error. Factors that hurt: history of repeated non-compliance, deliberate evasion, or failure to cooperate with GRA.

Double Taxation Agreements (DTAs) entered into by Ghana. A DTA is a bilateral tax treaty that prevents the same income being taxed twice — once in Ghana and once in the country of the other party. Countries with which Ghana has ratified DTAs include: United Kingdom, France, Germany, Belgium, Netherlands, Italy, South Africa, Switzerland, Czech Republic, Mauritius, Singapore, Denmark, Norway, Sweden, and others (verify current list at gra.gov.gh as new DTAs are periodically ratified).

DTAs typically cover: reduced withholding tax rates on dividends, interest and royalties, determination of tax residency for individuals and companies (tie-breaker rules), elimination or credit of double taxation on employment income, treatment of business profits of a permanent establishment. For example, under the Ghana-UK DTA, the WHT rate on dividends may be reduced from the domestic 8% to a lower treaty rate where the beneficial owner meets certain conditions.

How to claim DTA relief in Ghana: Apply to GRA in writing with evidence of tax residency in the treaty country and the relevant DTA article. GRA may issue a certificate allowing the withholder to apply the reduced treaty rate. Anti-avoidance: GRA may challenge DTA claims where there is abuse of treaty provisions (treaty shopping).

DTAs and Individuals Working in Ghana or Abroad. Ghanaian resident working abroad: A Ghanaian tax resident (one who spends more than 183 days in Ghana in a year or is domiciled in Ghana) is taxable on worldwide income. Income earned from employment abroad is taxable in Ghana. If the country where work is performed also taxes the income, the taxpayer can claim a foreign tax credit in Ghana for the tax paid abroad, up to the amount of Ghana tax on that income.

Foreign national working in Ghana: A non-resident who earns employment income in Ghana is taxed only on Ghana-source income. If the person's home country has a DTA with Ghana and the person spends less than 183 days in Ghana in the relevant period, they may be exempt from Ghana tax on employment income — subject to conditions (employer not resident in Ghana, salary not borne by a Ghana permanent establishment).

Directors fees: Always taxable in Ghana regardless of DTA if the company paying is a Ghana company. Short-term assignments: Some DTAs have specific provisions for short-term business visitors. Tax residency certificates: GRA can issue certificates of Ghana tax residency to Ghanaians for use in treaty partner countries.

How to Appeal a GRA Tax Assessment in Ghana. If you disagree with a tax assessment raised by GRA, you have a right of appeal. Process: 
Step 1: Within 30 days of receiving the assessment notice, lodge an Objection in writing with the Commissioner who issued the assessment. State clearly why you dispute the assessment and provide supporting evidence. 
Step 2: The Commissioner reviews the objection and issues a determination.

Step 3: If dissatisfied with the determination, appeal to the Revenue Appeals Board within 30 days of the determination. The Revenue Appeals Board is an independent tribunal. 
Step 4: From the Revenue Appeals Board, further appeal lies to the High Court on points of law within 30 days.

Step 5: Further appeal to the Court of Appeal and Supreme Court follows normal court procedures. Requirement to pay disputed tax: You are generally required to pay the undisputed portion of the assessment. GRA may agree to suspend payment of the disputed portion pending the outcome of the appeal if the taxpayer provides security. Failure to pay the undisputed portion while appealing may result in enforcement action. Alternative Dispute Resolution: GRA has an internal ADR mechanism — taxpayers can request ADR as an alternative to formal appeals.

Record Keeping Requirements Under Ghana Tax Law. Section 140 of the Income Tax Act 2015 (Act 896) requires taxpayers to keep records necessary to determine their tax liability. Records must be kept for a minimum of 6 years from the date of filing of the relevant return or from the date the tax was payable, whichever is later. What records to keep: Invoices issued and received. Bank statements. Cash books / petty cash records. Payroll records and P9 forms. Contracts and agreements.

Import and export documents. Capital asset purchase and disposal records. Loan agreements. Correspondence with GRA. VAT records including VAT invoices and return copies. Records may be kept in electronic form provided they are accessible, legible and can be produced to GRA on demand. An inadequate record keeping system does not prevent GRA from raising an estimated assessment based on best of judgment. Destruction of records before the 6-year period without GRA consent is an offence.

GRA Tax Audit Procedures. GRA conducts audits on taxpayers to verify accuracy of tax returns. Types of audits: Desk audit: review of returns and documents submitted without visiting the taxpayer. Field audit: GRA officers visit the taxpayer's premises. Comprehensive audit: covers all tax types for a given period. Issue-specific audit: focuses on a particular area (e.g. VAT input claims, transfer pricing). Notice of audit: GRA usually gives written notice before a field audit.

Taxpayer rights during audit: The right to request the identity of the audit team. The right to have a representative (tax consultant, accountant or lawyer) present. The right to respond to proposed adjustments before a final assessment is issued. The right of appeal against the final assessment. After the audit: GRA issues an audit report. If adjustments are proposed, a preliminary findings letter is sent to the taxpayer. The taxpayer has 30 days to respond. GRA then issues a final amended assessment.

Transfer pricing audit: Large companies with related party transactions may be subject to a dedicated TP audit — Ghana has Transfer Pricing Regulations 2020 requiring arm's length pricing and mandatory documentation.

Transfer Pricing Rules in Ghana. Transfer Pricing (TP) refers to the pricing of transactions between related parties (e.g. parent and subsidiary companies, associated enterprises). Ghana's TP Regulations were issued in 2012 and updated by the Transfer Pricing Regulations 2020 (L.I. 2,412). Key requirements: All controlled transactions (between related parties) must be priced at arm's length — i.e. at the price that would be charged between unrelated parties.

Companies with controlled transactions must prepare and maintain contemporaneous TP documentation. Master file: for large multinational groups. Local file: for each Ghana entity with related party transactions. Country-by-Country Report (CbCR): for ultimate parent entities of multinational groups with consolidated revenue above USD 850 million. Safe harbours: Some small taxpayers may qualify for simplified TP treatment.

Penalties for TP non-compliance: Up to GHS 15,000 for failure to submit documentation, plus adjustments and interest on underpaid tax. The Commissioner-General may make a TP adjustment if controlled transactions are not at arm's length. Advance Pricing Agreements: GRA has a provision for APAs (bilateral or unilateral) to provide certainty on TP treatment in advance.

Taxation of the Digital Economy in Ghana. GRA has increasingly focused on taxing income from digital and e-commerce activities. Ghanaian residents earning income from digital platforms (YouTube, TikTok, freelancing platforms like Upwork and Fiverr, social media influencing, online tutoring, cryptocurrency trading) are taxable on such income under the Income Tax Act 2015 (Act 896).

E-levy: Ghana introduced an Electronic Transfer Levy (E-Levy) of 1% on electronic money transfers above a daily threshold — however this levy has been subject to policy changes; verify current status at gra.gov.gh. Non-resident digital service providers: Companies providing digital services to Ghanaian consumers without a physical presence in Ghana may have an obligation to register for VAT under the reverse charge mechanism. Cryptocurrency: Gains from sale of crypto assets may be subject to CGT at 15%.

Income from crypto mining or staking may be subject to income tax. GRA is developing specific guidance on crypto taxation. GRA can access data from mobile money providers, banks and payment platforms to identify unreported digital income. Taxpayers engaged in digital commerce are advised to maintain clear records of all income received.

Employer PAYE Obligations in Full Detail. An employer in Ghana must: 1. Register as an employer with GRA within 30 days of commencing business or hiring the first employee. 2. Obtain and complete a PAYE registration form. 3. Deduct PAYE from each employee's gross salary every month using the correct graduated rates. 4. Deduct the employee's SSNIT contribution (5.5% of gross salary) and remit it to SSNIT (not GRA). 5. Make the employer's SSNIT contribution (13% of gross salary) to SSNIT. 6.

Remit the deducted PAYE to GRA by the 15th of the following month. 7. File a monthly PAYE return (Form R1) showing each employee, their salary, PAYE deducted. 8. At year-end (December or the end of the employment year) issue a P9 form (End of Year Certificate) to each employee by 31 January. 9. File an annual PAYE reconciliation return with GRA by 31 March. New employees: Obtain a Tax Deduction Card from the employee (issued by GRA).

If the employee has no card, apply 5% withholding on employment income until a card is obtained. Termination: On termination of employment, final PAYE settlement must be made. Redundancy payments are taxable to the extent they exceed a quarter of total terminal emoluments.

Tax on Rental Income in Ghana. Landlords who rent out property in Ghana are subject to tax on rental income. For individual landlords: The tenant (if a company or government institution) must withhold 8% WHT on rent payments and remit to GRA. This 8% is a final tax — the landlord does not need to include the rental income in an annual return provided WHT has been correctly deducted. If the tenant is an individual (not a company), no WHT is deducted.

The landlord must declare the rental income in their annual tax return and pay income tax at the graduated individual rates. For companies renting out property: The 8% WHT deducted by the tenant is a creditable tax (not final). The company includes rental income in its CIT return and credits the WHT against its CIT liability. Advance rent: If rent is paid in advance for multiple years (common in Ghana), the landlord is taxed on the full advance amount received.

Best practice is to spread recognition over the rental period. Property owners are advised to register their rental properties with GRA. GRA has been conducting rental income compliance exercises, especially in residential areas of Accra, Kumasi, Tema and Takoradi.

Tax Incentives and Exemptions for Agriculture in Ghana. Ghana has deliberately offered tax incentives to encourage agricultural investment. Corporate tax exemptions: Companies engaged in farming, animal husbandry and fishing are exempt from income tax for the first 5 years. After the exemption period, a reduced CIT rate of 20% applies. Tree crops (cocoa, rubber, palm) benefit from longer exemption periods — first 10 years. Export of non-traditional agricultural products qualifies for a reduced 8% CIT rate.

VAT exemptions: Unprocessed foodstuffs locally grown or caught are VAT-exempt. Agricultural inputs — seeds, fertilisers, pesticides, and agricultural machinery imported for farming — are VAT-exempt or attract 0% VAT. PAYE: Agricultural workers on large farms are subject to normal PAYE rules if employed formally. Smallholder farmers are generally not required to file returns unless income exceeds taxable thresholds.

GRA works with COCOBOD (for cocoa farmers) to administer any applicable taxes at the farm gate level. Note: Processing of agricultural products (milling, canning, cooking oil production) may not qualify for the agricultural exemption — this is treated as manufacturing.

Ghana Free Zones and Tax Benefits. Free Zone enterprises registered under the Ghana Free Zones Authority (GFZA) enjoy significant tax incentives. CIT exemption: 0% tax for the first 10 years of operation. After 10 years: 15% CIT rate (vs 25% standard). Import duty exemption: No import duties on raw materials, equipment, machinery, spare parts and other inputs used in free zone production. VAT exemption: Sales from Free Zone enterprises to other Free Zone enterprises or for export are exempt from VAT.

WHT: Dividends paid to foreign investors in Free Zones are exempt from WHT. Conditions for Free Zone benefits: The enterprise must export at least 70% of its goods or services. Domestic sales (to the non-free zone economy) are limited to 30% and subject to normal customs duty and VAT on that portion. The enterprise must maintain separate accounts for free zone and non-free zone activities. GFZA and GRA jointly monitor compliance.
Any violation of the 70% export requirement can result in withdrawal of free zone status and back-assessment of taxes.

Petroleum Tax Regime in Ghana. Ghana discovered oil in commercial quantities in 2007 (Jubilee field) and oil production began in 2010. The petroleum tax regime is governed by the Petroleum (Exploration and Production) Act 2016 (Act 919) and the Petroleum Income Tax Act (PITA). Key tax elements for oil and gas companies: CIT: 35% on petroleum income (ring-fenced per field/petroleum agreement).

Royalties: paid to the Government as a production share (typically 5% to 12.5% of gross production depending on the field agreement). Additional Oil Entitlement (AOE): triggered when the contractor's after-tax rate of return exceeds a certain threshold — the Government takes additional oil. Carried interest: The Government (through GNPC) carries a participating interest in petroleum operations. Capital allowances: Exploration costs are capitalised and amortised; development costs allowances vary.

Surface rentals: Annual fees per block. Training obligations: Foreign companies must train Ghanaian employees. Local content requirements: Governed separately by the Local Content Regulations. GNPC (Ghana National Petroleum Corporation) is the national oil company and partner in all petroleum agreements. GRA works with the Ministry of Energy and GNPC to administer petroleum taxes.

Why Taxes Matter in Ghana — A GRA Message. Taxes are the lifeblood of Ghana's development. When you pay your taxes, you contribute to: Building and maintaining roads, bridges, schools and hospitals. Paying salaries of teachers, nurses, doctors, police officers and soldiers. Providing social protection to the poor and vulnerable. Funding national security and public safety. Servicing Ghana's public debt. Over 80% of Ghana's domestic revenue comes from taxes collected by GRA.

Ghana uses a self-assessment system for most taxes — this means the taxpayer calculates their own tax and files returns voluntarily. GRA's role is to educate taxpayers, facilitate compliance, and enforce the law against those who deliberately evade tax. Tax avoidance (legal minimisation of tax using lawful means) is different from tax evasion (illegal non-disclosure or fraud). Avoidance is legal; evasion is a crime.

GRA encourages voluntary compliance and provides multiple channels to pay and file including mobile money, online portal, and GRA offices across Ghana. Ghana's national slogan for tax: Paying Tax is Paying for Ghana.

Mandatory Levies Charged Alongside VAT in Ghana. Ghana Health Insurance Levy (NHIL): 2.5% charged on all standard-rated VAT supplies. The NHIL is used to fund the National Health Insurance Scheme (NHIS) administered by NHIA. Ghana Education Trust Fund Levy (GETFund Levy): 2.5% charged on all standard-rated VAT supplies. The GETFund Levy funds the Ghana Education Trust Fund which supports education infrastructure.

COVID-19 Health Recovery Levy: 1% introduced in 2021 to help recover from the pandemic's economic impact. This levy applies on VAT-taxable supplies. Note: as at the last update these levies are charged separately from VAT on the tax invoice but collected and remitted alongside VAT by the VAT-registered supplier. Total rate on standard-rated supplies = 15% VAT + 2.5% NHIL + 2.5% GETFund + 1% COVID Levy = 21% (exclusive basis). Actual effective rate on a price-inclusive basis is approximately 21.9%.

The levies are calculated on the same base as VAT (the taxable value of the supply). All these levies are remitted to GRA as part of the monthly VAT return. No input credit is available for NHIL and GETFund levies (unlike VAT where input credit is available).

How to Pay Taxes Using Mobile Money and Other Methods in Ghana. GRA has integrated multiple payment channels to make tax payment convenient. Mobile Money: You can pay taxes directly from your MTN Mobile Money, Vodafone Cash, or AirtelTigo Money account. Use the GRA short code or visit the GRA payment page on the mobile money app. The payment reference number from your GRA return is required. GhIPSS Instant Pay: Bank-to-bank online transfers via the GhIPSS platform.

Bank payment: Visit any of the designated commercial banks with your GRA payment slip and make a cash or cheque deposit into the GRA account. GRA portal payment: Log into taxpayerportal.gra.gov.gh, complete the return, and pay by debit/credit card or Mobile Money from the portal. GRA has also installed Point of Sale (POS) terminals at some GRA offices. Please note: always keep your payment receipt and confirmation as proof of payment.

If there is a discrepancy between your payment and the GRA system, you will need the payment reference number to trace it. For large payments, RTGS (Real Time Gross Settlement) bank transfer is recommended. Payments by cash are accepted at GRA offices. All payments must reference your TIN and the relevant return period.

Installment Payment Arrangement for Outstanding Taxes. If you have a large tax debt you cannot pay immediately, GRA may agree to an installment payment plan (also called a payment arrangement or tax payment agreement). How to apply: 
Step 1: Contact your GRA district office or relationship manager and declare your tax debt. 
Step 2: Provide evidence of your current financial position — bank statements, income statement, cash flow projection.

Step 3: Propose a realistic installment schedule (e.g. monthly payments over 6 to 12 months). 
Step 4: GRA reviews and may negotiate the terms.

Step 5: Once agreed, the installment plan is formalised in writing. You must honour the plan — failure to keep up with payments may result in GRA terminating the plan and enforcing the full debt. Interest: Interest typically continues to accrue on the outstanding balance during the installment period unless waived as part of the arrangement. GRA may require a director's guarantee or other security for large debts. Having an agreed installment plan may help qualify for a Tax Clearance Certificate. This is subject to GRA discretion.

Tax Offences and Prosecution Under Ghana Tax Law. The Revenue Administration Act 2016 (Act 915) and the Income Tax Act 2015 (Act 896) provide for criminal offences relating to tax. Offences include: Wilful failure to file a return. Making a false or misleading statement in a return. Wilful evasion of tax. Obstruction of a GRA officer. Failure to maintain records. Providing false information to obtain a TIN or registration. Using a false TIN. Assisting another person to evade tax.

Penalties: Fines and/or imprisonment up to 3 to 5 years depending on the offence. Civil penalties (money fines) are separate from criminal prosecution — both can apply. GRA has a Tax Investigation Division that investigates suspected tax fraud. Serious cases are referred to the Attorney General for prosecution. GRA may also share information with the Financial Intelligence Centre (FIC) where tax evasion is linked to money laundering.

Whistleblowers: The Whistleblower Act 2006 (Act 720) provides protection and rewards for persons who report tax evasion or fraud to GRA. Rewards can be up to 10% of the recovered tax. Reports can be made to GRA in confidence.

GRA Office Locations and Contact Details. GRA Head Office: Starlets 91 Road, Opposite Accra Sports Stadium, Accra. Postal address: P.O. Box 2,202, Accra. Helpline: 0800-900-110 (toll-free). Email: info@gra.gov.gh. Website: gra.gov.gh. Taxpayer Portal: taxpayerportal.gra.gov.gh. Regional Offices: Greater Accra: Accra Main, Tema, Adabraka, Kaneshie, Madina, Teshie-Nungua, Accra New Town, Adenta, Airport, Lapaz, Nsawam, Dodowa.

Ashanti: Kumasi Main, Oforikrom, Suame, Adum, Bantama, Asokwa, Manhyia, Ejisu, Mampong. Western: Takoradi, Sekondi, Tarkwa, Axim. Central: Cape Coast, Winneba, Mankessim, Swedru. Eastern: Koforidua, Nkawkaw, Suhum, Kade. Volta: Ho, Aflao, Hohoe, Keta. Northern: Tamale Main, Sagnarigu, Savelugu. Upper East: Bolgatanga, Bawku, Navrongo. Upper West: Wa, Jirapa. Oti, Savannah, North East, Bono, Bono East, Ahafo regions: GRA has offices at the regional capital in each region.

Large Taxpayer Office (LTO): Located at Accra Main, handles taxpayers with annual turnover above a set threshold. Port offices: GRA Customs has offices at Tema Port, Takoradi Port, Kotoka International Airport, and land border crossings.

Tax Guide for Expatriates in Ghana. An expatriate is a non-Ghanaian national working in Ghana. Tax residency: An expatriate becomes a tax resident of Ghana if they are present in Ghana for 183 days or more in any 12-month period. Resident expatriates are taxed on their Ghana-source income. Employment income: Salaries and wages earned from work performed in Ghana are taxable in Ghana regardless of where the salary is paid.

Salary paid offshore to avoid Ghana PAYE is still taxable — the Ghana employer or the non-resident employer with a PE in Ghana must still operate PAYE. Benefits in kind: Housing, vehicles, school fees and other benefits provided by the employer to an expatriate are included in employment income and taxed accordingly. The taxable value of employer-provided housing is 10% of employment income; employer-provided vehicle is 5%.

Relief under DTAs: An expatriate from a country that has a DTA with Ghana may claim exemption from Ghana tax on employment income if they spend less than 183 days in Ghana in the relevant period and their employer is not a Ghana resident and their salary is not borne by a Ghana PE. Work permit holders: All expatriates with work permits should have a TIN. GRA has a dedicated expatriate processing unit.

Tax Treatment of Social Enterprises and Hybrid Organisations. Ghana's tax law distinguishes between: Charitable organisations (pure non-profit, applying for exemption): May obtain income tax exemption under Section 97 of the Income Tax Act. Social enterprises (hybrid organisations combining commercial activity with social purpose): Generally not automatically exempt — commercial income is taxable even if profits are reinvested for social purposes.

Religious bodies: Churches, mosques and religious organisations are generally exempt from income tax on tithes, offerings and donations received as part of religious activities. However, commercial activities of religious organisations (schools, hospitals, businesses) are taxable. A religious organisation running a school must pay tax on the school's profits unless the school qualifies for an educational exemption.

PAYE: All employees of charities, religious bodies, NGOs and social enterprises are subject to PAYE on their salaries — there is no exemption for employment income. VAT: If a charity or religious body makes taxable supplies above GHS 200,000 it must register for VAT. GRA may request audited accounts of any organisation claiming exempt status.

Tax on Gambling, Betting and Lottery Income in Ghana. Sports betting companies and other gaming operators are required to pay: Corporate Income Tax on their profits at 25%. VAT may apply to certain gaming services — check with GRA. Winnings from lotteries, betting and gaming: Lottery and betting winnings above GHS 3,500 per winning are subject to a 5% withholding tax. The gaming operator deducts the 5% at source before paying out to the winner.

This 5% is a final tax — winners do not need to include betting winnings in their income tax return. The gaming operator files a monthly WHT return and remits the 5% deducted to GRA. Endorsement income from brand deals linked to gaming: taxed as employment income or self-employment income depending on the taxpayer's status. Regulation of gaming operators: Licensed by the Gaming Commission of Ghana. GRA and the Gaming Commission share compliance information.
Online gaming platforms operating from outside Ghana targeting Ghanaian consumers may be required to register for VAT under reverse charge rules — policy evolving as at 2024.

Tax on Deceased Estates in Ghana. When a person dies, their estate (assets and liabilities) does not immediately cease to have tax obligations. The administrator or executor of the estate is responsible for: Filing outstanding tax returns for the deceased up to the date of death. Paying any outstanding taxes of the deceased. Obtaining a Tax Clearance Certificate from GRA (sometimes required by Probate Courts for granting Letters of Administration).

Income earned by the estate after the date of death (e.g. rent, interest, dividends) is taxable income of the estate. The administrator must register the estate as a taxpayer with GRA. Estate income is taxed at the individual progressive rates. Inheritance: Ghana does not have a specific inheritance tax or estate duty. The recipient of inherited assets does not pay income tax on the inheritance as such — but any income subsequently generated from inherited assets is taxable.

If inherited assets are sold, CGT may apply. Gifts to heirs during the lifetime of the deceased may be subject to gift tax rules. Lawyers handling estate administration are advised to involve a tax professional to ensure proper GRA compliance.

Q: I just started a new job. When does my employer start deducting PAYE? A: Your employer should start deducting PAYE from your very first salary. There is no grace period. If your employer is not deducting PAYE, you should remind them of their legal obligation.

Q: My salary is GHS 2,000 per month. Do I pay tax? A: With a monthly salary of GHS 2,000 you will pay some income tax depending on your personal reliefs. The first GHS 490 per month is taxed at 0%, and the remaining portions are taxed at graduated rates. Actual PAYE depends on what reliefs you have claimed.

Q: I earn tips at a restaurant. Are tips taxable? A: Yes. Tips and gratuities received in the course of employment are part of employment income and subject to PAYE. The employer should include tips in the PAYE calculation. 

Q: I received a bonus at work. Is that taxed differently? A: No. A bonus is employment income and is taxed at the same graduated PAYE rates as your regular salary in the month it is paid. This may push you into a higher band for that month.

Q: I work two jobs. How does PAYE work for me? A: You must declare your second income to both employers and to GRA. GRA will issue a Tax Deduction Card specifying the correct rates for each employer. Without disclosure, each employer applies single-income PAYE rates, and at year-end you will likely owe additional tax.

Q: I resigned mid-year. Does my new employer start PAYE from scratch? A: Your new employer calculates PAYE on your earnings from them. However your total income tax liability for the year includes income from both jobs. Your previous employer should give you a P9 form showing income earned and PAYE deducted, which you use for your annual reconciliation.

Q: I received an income tax return (refund) request letter from GRA. What does that mean? A: GRA may have identified that you have been over-taxed (PAYE deducted exceeded your correct liability) or that you have not been filing returns. If it is a return request it means GRA wants you to file a return. If it is a refund, GRA owes you money. Visit your local GRA office or call 0800-900-110 for clarification.

Q: I sold my house and made a profit. Do I pay tax on it? A: If the house was your main home and you have lived there for at least 3 years, the gain is exempt from CGT. If it was not your main home (e.g. an investment property or second home), you pay CGT at 15% on the profit. The return and payment are due within 30 days of the sale.

Q: I bought shares on the Ghana Stock Exchange and made a profit. Is that taxable? A: No. Gains from listed shares on the Ghana Stock Exchange are exempt from CGT to encourage investment in the capital market. Dividends received from listed companies are subject to 8% WHT which is a final tax.

Q: I received a cash gift from my uncle who lives abroad. Is it taxable? A: Gifts from family members are generally exempt from income tax in Ghana. However if the amounts are very large and regular, GRA could potentially argue it constitutes income. For formal gift transfers of significant assets, consult a tax advisor.

Q: I own a plot of land I have not developed. Do I pay any tax on it? A: No income or capital gains tax arises until you sell or rent out the land. Property rates (local government levy) may apply — that is administered by the local Metropolitan, Municipal or District Assembly not GRA.

Q: My business has not started making money yet. Do I still need to file a tax return? A: Yes. Once registered for a tax type you must file returns even if you have no income (nil return). For income tax, if you had a full year with no income you may not owe tax but you should still file to avoid late filing penalties in some cases — check with GRA.

Q: I made a loss this year. Can I carry it forward? A: Yes. A business can carry forward a tax loss and offset it against taxable income in future years. Losses can generally be carried forward for up to 5 years under the Income Tax Act. Losses from mining operations may have different rules.

Q: I am a landlord and my tenant is an individual. No WHT has been deducted from my rent. What do I do? A: If your tenant is an individual (not a company or government body) they have no obligation to deduct WHT. In this case you must declare your rental income in your annual income tax return and pay tax at the graduated individual rates. You should register with GRA as a rental income earner.

Q: I run a chop bar (local restaurant). Do I need to register for VAT? A: Only if your annual sales exceed GHS 200,000. Below this threshold VAT registration is voluntary. As a chop bar, some supplies may be exempt (unprocessed food items).

Q: My company pays dividends to shareholders. What tax applies? A: You must withhold 8% from each dividend payment and remit to GRA by the 15th of the following month. Issue WHT certificates to shareholders. 

Q: I received a dividend from my company. Do I include it in my income tax return? A: The 8% WHT on dividends is a final tax. You do not include dividend income in your income tax return. However keep the WHT certificate as your proof of tax paid.

Q: My business partner and I split profits equally. How are we taxed? A: If you operate as a partnership (not a company), each partner is taxed individually on their share of the partnership profits at the individual graduated rates. The partnership itself does not pay CIT. If you operate as a company, the company pays CIT and then shareholders pay 8% WHT on dividends.

Q: I want to close my company. What are the tax steps? A: You must file all outstanding returns and pay all outstanding taxes. De-register from VAT if registered. Obtain a Tax Clearance Certificate. Inform GRA in writing of the closure. Cancel any PAYE and WHT registrations. The final return covers up to the date of winding up. Any assets distributed to shareholders on liquidation may be treated as a dividend.

Q: I am a nurse working for a private hospital. My salary is GHS 4,000 a month. What reliefs can I claim? A: You can claim reliefs such as marriage relief (if married), child relief (if you have children), and old age relief (if you are 60 or above). Apply for them using Form IT1 from GRA.

Q: I pay private health insurance for myself and my family. Is that deductible? A: Life insurance premiums may qualify for a relief up to 10% of assessable income. Private health insurance is slightly different from life insurance — confirm the current treatment with GRA as the rules have been debated.

Q: I have a mortgage on my home. Can I deduct mortgage interest? A: Under Ghana tax law, interest on a mortgage for your principal private residence is not an allowable deduction against employment income. Mortgage interest is only deductible if the property is a business asset or rental property generating income.

Q: I sponsored my child through university. Can I claim any relief for education expenses? A: There is no education expense relief for sponsoring your own children's education at university. The dependent child relief (GHS 1,200 per child) applies if the child is a dependant in full-time education. There is a training/education relief for professional development of the taxpayer themselves.

Q: I run a printing business. My annual revenue is GHS 350,000. Do I need to register for VAT? A: Yes. Your revenue exceeds the GHS 200,000 VAT registration threshold. You must register for VAT immediately. Apply online at taxpayerportal.gra.gov.gh or visit a GRA VAT office. 

Q: Can I charge VAT before my VAT registration is approved? A: No. You must not charge VAT to customers until you are officially registered and have received your VAT Certificate. Charging VAT without registration is an offence.

Q: I am VAT registered. My supplier is not VAT registered. Can I claim input VAT from their invoice? A: No. You can only claim input VAT on purchases from VAT-registered suppliers who issue a valid VAT invoice. If your supplier is not VAT registered, they should not be charging VAT and you cannot claim input on their invoices.

Q: I provide both VAT-exempt and standard-rated services. How do I handle input VAT? A: You are a partially exempt business. You must apportion your input VAT between taxable and exempt activities using an approved attribution method. The portion attributable to exempt supplies is not recoverable. This is called the partial exemption calculation.

Q: My output VAT is less than my input VAT. What happens? A: You have a VAT credit. You can carry it forward to the next period or apply to GRA for a refund. Refund claims are subject to GRA verification.

Q: I need my TIN urgently for a bank account opening. How quickly can I get it? A: You can get your TIN on the same day by visiting any GRA office with your Ghana Card. Online self-service TIN issuance at taxpayerportal.gra.gov.gh can also be instant in some cases.

Q: Can I check if someone has a valid TIN? A: TINs are personal. GRA does not publicly disclose another person's TIN. However businesses can verify if a TIN provided to them is genuine by calling GRA or using the portal's TIN verification feature.

Q: My company is registered in another ECOWAS country but we have operations in Ghana. Do we need a Ghana TIN? A: Yes. Any entity earning income in Ghana or employing people in Ghana needs a Ghana TIN. Even if you do not have a formal legal entity in Ghana, if you have a permanent establishment (fixed place of business) you must register.

Q: I keep getting notices from GRA saying I have outstanding returns. I never registered for any tax. What is happening? A: GRA may have registered you based on information from third parties (employers, business registrations, property transactions). Visit the GRA office that sent the notice to clarify your tax registration status and resolve any outstanding issues.

Q: I ordered a laptop from an online store abroad. Will I pay customs duty? A: Yes. Goods imported into Ghana regardless of how they are ordered (online or otherwise) are subject to customs duty. Electronics like laptops attract duty rates under the ECOWAS CET. Your courier or freight forwarder will handle the customs process and charge you the applicable duties plus their fees.

Q: I brought goods back from my trip abroad. Do I declare them? A: Every traveller must declare goods above the passenger's allowance threshold (typically USD 500 equivalent). Goods above this threshold are subject to duty. Undeclared dutiable goods may be seized by GRA Customs.

Q: I am a trader and I want to import goods. Do I need any special permits? A: Some goods require import permits from specific government agencies (e.g. food and drugs require FDA approval, pharmaceuticals require UNIPASS clearance, electronics may need NCA approval). GRA Customs will require these permits before clearing the goods. General traders need a TIN and may need to register as an importer with GRA Customs.

Q: What happens if I disagree with the customs value GRA uses? A: You can formally challenge the customs value by lodging an objection. GRA Customs must give reasons for using a different value. If unresolved, the dispute can be escalated to the Revenue Appeals Board.

Q: I have not filed my tax returns for 3 years. What should I do? A: You should come forward voluntarily and file all outstanding returns as soon as possible. Voluntary compliance before GRA discovers the non-compliance is viewed more favourably. GRA may impose late filing penalties (GHS 500 per month per return type) but may consider reducing penalties if you approach them proactively. Call 0800-900-110 or visit your nearest GRA office.

Q: GRA has issued me a penalty I cannot afford to pay. What are my options? A: You can apply in writing to the Commissioner of your tax office for a penalty waiver, explaining your circumstances. Alternatively you can negotiate an installment payment plan for the penalties and taxes combined.

Q: I paid my taxes but I have no receipt. GRA is saying I still owe. What should I do? A: Gather your payment evidence — bank debit notice, mobile money transaction SMS, or bank teller receipt. Visit the GRA office with the evidence and request a reconciliation of your account. GRA officers can trace payments using the reference number on the payment.

Q: I got a court summons from GRA. What should I do? A: Take this very seriously and engage a tax lawyer or advisor immediately. Do not ignore the summons. Contact the GRA Large Taxpayer Office or your district office to understand the issue and attempt to resolve it before court.

Q: I have 3 employees. How do I register as an employer with GRA? A: Visit any GRA office and request to register as an employer/withholding agent. You need your company TIN, Certificate of Incorporation, and details of your employees. You can also register online via the taxpayer portal.

Q: Can I pay my employees net of tax (employer bears the PAYE)? A: Technically the employer can agree to bear the employee's PAYE, but this creates a tax grossing-up situation where the benefit (the employer paying tax) is itself taxable. This creates complex calculations. GRA expects PAYE to be operated in the normal manner.

Q: One of my employees resigned in September. Do I file a final PAYE return for them? A: File the PAYE return for September including that employee's income and deductions. Issue the employee their P9 form covering January to September (or whatever the employment period was).

Q: I am a domestic employer (I have house helps). Do I pay PAYE for them? A: If you pay a domestic worker (house help, driver, gardener) a salary and their income exceeds the zero-rated threshold (first GHS 490/month is tax-free in 2024 rates) you should technically operate PAYE. In practice this is rarely enforced for small domestic arrangements but legally the obligation exists.

Q: I do graphic design on Fiverr and earn in USD. How do I declare this in Ghana? A: Convert your annual USD earnings to Ghana cedis at the prevailing exchange rate. Include the converted amount as your self-employment income in your annual income tax return (Form IT1). File by 30 April. Deductible expenses: internet, software subscriptions, computer depreciation — these reduce your taxable profit.

Q: I am an influencer on Instagram and brands pay me. What tax do I owe? A: Influencer fees and endorsement income are taxable. Register for a TIN, keep records of all payments received, and file an annual return. Endorsement fees from Ghanaian brands may attract 10% WHT at source — your client should deduct this.

Q: I run an online store selling Ghana handmade goods to buyers worldwide. Any tax advice? A: Sales to foreign buyers (export) are zero-rated for VAT if you are VAT registered. Your income (profits from the business) is subject to income tax. If your annual revenue exceeds GHS 200,000 you must register for VAT. Keep records of all transactions.

Q: Do I have to declare my crypto income to GRA? A: Yes. Cryptocurrency profits are taxable in Ghana. Gains from disposal of crypto are subject to CGT at 15%. Regular trading income may be treated as business income. GRA is developing specific crypto guidance — monitor gra.gov.gh for updates.

Key Tax Terms Used in Ghana — A Glossary. Assessable Income: Total income from all sources before deduction of personal reliefs. Chargeable Income: Assessable income minus allowable reliefs and deductions — the amount on which tax is actually computed. Commissioner-General: The head of GRA — appointed by the President. CIT: Corporate Income Tax. Creditable WHT: Withholding Tax that is not final — it can be credited against the taxpayer's annual income tax liability.

Final WHT: Withholding Tax that discharges the taxpayer's full liability on that income — they do not need to include it in their annual return. Input VAT: VAT paid on purchases by a VAT-registered business. Output VAT: VAT charged on sales by a VAT-registered business. Net VAT: Output VAT minus Input VAT — the amount payable to GRA. P9 Form: Certificate issued by employer to employee showing annual emoluments and PAYE deducted. PAYE: Pay As You Earn — income tax deducted at source by employer.

Permanent Establishment (PE): A fixed place of business through which a foreign enterprise carries on business in Ghana. Provisional Tax: Advance payment of income tax made by self-employed persons and companies during the tax year. TIN: Taxpayer Identification Number — 11-digit unique identifier assigned by GRA. Turnover Tax: A simplified tax regime for very small businesses based on gross turnover. WHT: Withholding Tax — tax deducted at source by a payer before paying the recipient.
Zero-rated Supply: A supply subject to VAT at 0% — the supplier can still claim input VAT. Exempt Supply: A supply not subject to VAT — no VAT charged, and no input VAT claimable on related costs.

Key Tax Legislation Administered by GRA. Income Tax Act, 2015 (Act 896): Main act governing income tax for individuals and companies. Amended by Income Tax (Amendment) Acts: Act 941 (2016), Act 956 (2017), Act 973 (2018), Act 1,007 (2020), Act 1,066 (2021), and subsequent amendments. Value Added Tax Act, 2013 (Act 870): Governs VAT, NHIL, GETFund Levy. Revenue Administration Act, 2016 (Act 915): Governs GRA's powers of administration, audit, assessment, enforcement and appeals.

Customs Act, 2015 (Act 891): Governs customs and excise. Excise Duty Act, 2014 (Act 878): Governs excise duties on specified goods. Communications Service Tax Act, 2008 (Act 754): Governs CST. Transfer Pricing Regulations, 2020 (L.I. 2,412): Governs TP documentation and arm's length requirements. Petroleum Income Tax Act (PITA): Governs tax on petroleum income. Ghana Investment Promotion Centre Act, 2013 (Act 865): Investment incentives including tax holidays.

Free Zones Act, 1995 (Act 504) as amended: Free Zone incentives. Whistleblower Act, 2006 (Act 720): Protection and reward for tax fraud reporters. Revenue Appeals Board: Created under the Revenue Administration Act to hear tax appeals. All acts available at gra.gov.gh or Parliament House Library.

Q: What is the difference between SSNIT and GRA? A: SSNIT (Social Security and National Insurance Trust) is a pension fund that collects social security contributions for workers' retirement. GRA (Ghana Revenue Authority) is the government tax collection authority. SSNIT contributions are not taxes — they go into a pension fund for your future benefit. GRA taxes fund government services.

Q: My employer deducts both SSNIT and PAYE. Is that correct? A: Yes. The employee contributes 5.5% of gross salary to SSNIT (pension contribution, not a tax). The employer also contributes 13% to SSNIT. PAYE is computed on the gross salary MINUS the 5.5% SSNIT deduction — so your SSNIT contribution reduces your tax base.

Q: If I pay SSNIT my whole career do I still need to pay income tax? A: Yes. SSNIT contributions and income tax are separate obligations. SSNIT is for pension; income tax funds public services. Both apply.
